segments.text: So I'd like to go ahead with complex reasoning.
And we've talked a little bit about reasoning
and language models up until now.
And so I'm going to be talking about stuff
that we didn't talk about yet.
This might be a little bit short because of that,
because I'm not talking about programs,
because we talked about that in the code generation class.
And we already talked a little bit about some of the basics here.
But if we have time at the end,
I'd be happy to discuss free form also.
So what is reasoning?
The basic idea is using evidence and logic
to arrive at conclusions and make judgments.
And what is it in language models
is a little bit less clear.
But if we talk about it kind of like
from the philosophical standpoint, there are two varieties
of this.
One is formal reasoning.
And formal reasoning is mostly based on strict truth values.
So it's kind of like you can definitely say this is true.
You can definitely say this is not true.
And in real life, there's very little actual formal reasoning
outside of like, for example, mathematics
or maybe algorithms, computer science,
and other things like that.
And then separately from that, we have informal reasoning
based on experience and intuition.
And actually, this was rather elusive
until large language models.
People were working on it, but it was really hard.
And this is like one of the big breakthroughs,
I think, of the past few years.
I should note that this paper here, Huang and Chun,
is a kind of review survey paper
of reasoning in large language models.
It's on the references.
So if you're interested, you can take a look at that too.
But there's three kinds of reasoning.
There's many kinds of reasoning, but there's
three kinds of reasoning in particular
that I'd like to talk about from the point of view of today.
And the first one is deductive reasoning.
Ind deductive reasoning is basically using logic
to go from a premise to a conclusion.
And this is largely what people, not entirely,
but largely what people talk about when they think about formal
reasoning.
And so basically, you have several premises.
Like all mammals have kidneys and all whales are mammals.
And then from this, you can go to all whales have kidneys.
And separately, there's inductive reasoning.
And inductive reasoning is from observation,
predict a likely conclusion or predict a likely kind
of generalized conclusion.
So this is one example.
When we see a creature with wings, it is usually a bird.
We see a creature with wings.
The creature is likely to be a bird.
So it's kind of, this is kind of like a soft version
of deduction.
Another common thing is like every single creature
I have seen with wings is a bird.
And then you can kind of induce that all creatures with wings
are birds.
But that might not be true.
It's not necessarily logically entailed.
But you make that kind of logical conclusion
without it being formally correct or verifiable.
And then the final one is abductive reasoning.
And so this is from an observation.
We predict the most likely explanation.
And so for example, if we have something
like the car cannot start, and there
is a puddle liquid under the engine,
then we might have a likely explanation
that the car has a leak in the radiator.
So we're going from kind of the car, you know,
these things.
And then we try to predict the reason why this happens.
So we're trying to predict like reverse causal links,
essentially.
There's other types of reasoning
that I'm not going to talk about as much,
like analogical reasoning and things like this.
But these are the three main ones
I want to talk about today.
So one thing I should point out is even in philosophy,
or even when you read descriptions
about these various types of reasoning,
the types are a little bit vague.
So take these as general directions and not strict rules,
because which falls under which category
also can be a little bit unclear
according to various definitions.
Cool.
So first, before getting into formal reasoning methods,
before getting into the bulk of the talk,
which is going to be about LLMs,
I want to talk about some pre-LLLM reasoning methods.
And the first one is kind of like formal reasoning
within computational semantics.
And this has been around for a really long time.
It's also kind of what powered the things
that worked over knowledge bases and other things like this.
And the way it works is it does
is derivational reasoning by, sorry, I can't read that in the back,
by starting out with certain premises
and getting to final conclusions.
So there's ways that you can write this.
So I think you might have seen this in another math class
or something.
But we have symbols like all and exist.
Yeah, we have things like all and exist.
Like all x-die means like everything has died.
And this implies that Mia and Z have died.
So yeah, this is a, yeah, actually,
maybe I'll not go through this one.
And let me go through, go to this one.
So like, there would be something like for all x mammal,
all x implies have x, can be or something like that.
And then you would have other rules.
And you can go through derivations and other things like this.
My favorite reference for this is this Blackburn and Buzz book
right here.
It's really well written.
And it has lots of good examples.
It also explains how you go through derivations and other stuff
like that.
And actually, neural networks can do this variety of reasoning
through chain of thought and other things
I'm going to talk about today.
But it's a very rough approximation.
And it doesn't work particularly well for saying all people
are of things that apply to all people
or things that apply to sets or other things like this.
So within Prologue, you could take a knowledge base
and ask the knowledge base like, do all people who work
at CMU as professors have a PhD?
And you could actually examine that based on the knowledge
base, whereas even if you had a language model that had
access to everybody's CVs, it wouldn't necessarily
be able to answer that question.
And especially wouldn't be able to answer that question
if you were, wouldn't be able to answer that question
if there were multiple steps.
So did all people who are working at CMU
get their PhD after 1990 or something like that?
So in the answer to that is obviously no.
But this would be able to find the counter evidence to that,
whereas LMS would not be guaranteed to be able to do that.
So I think this is really a nice thing to know.
But there's a couple of problems with it.
The first thing is this really only traffic
and strictly true or strictly false statements.
And that's a really big issue.
So if anything soft, you start this formal reasoning
starts breaking down.
The second thing, which actually is a really big problem,
is once you start dealing with more complex things,
you don't realize it, but there's always exceptions
and exceptions to exceptions and other things like that.
And actually it becomes very computationally
expensive to prove anything that's kind of non-trivial.
And so because of that, I'm not actually
going to cover it in the lecture today.
But recently, there are search algorithms
through proof spaces that use neural models
to do to speed up the search by picking
the best and most promising hypotheses.
And for example, Sean Wellack here at CMU.
